name: Xibalba
description: Xibalba, meaning "Place of Fear," is the name of the underworld in Maya mythology. Ruled by the death lords, Hun-Came ("One Death") and Vucub-Came ("Seven Death"), it is a treacherous realm filled with trials, traps, and deceits. The entrance to Xibalba was traditionally believed to be located in a cave system in Alta Verapaz, Guatemala. The underworld is described as a vast and complex place, with numerous houses or chambers designed to torment and test those who enter. These include the House of Darkness, the House of Knives, and the House of Bats, each posing unique dangers. Popol Vuh, the sacred text of the K'iche' Maya, provides the most comprehensive account of Xibalba and the challenges faced by heroes who dared to venture within its borders. Overcoming these trials was crucial for achieving rebirth and restoring balance to the cosmos. Xibalba's significance lies in its role as a place of transformation and a representation of the cyclical nature of life and death, central to Maya cosmology.
subtitle: The Maya Underworld: Place of Fear
location: Beneath the surface of the Earth; possibly Alta Verapaz, Guatemala
significance: Represents the realm of death, the underworld in Maya cosmology, and a place of trials and transformation for heroes.

keyMyths.title: The Descent of Hunahpu and Xbalanque
keyMyths.description: The Hero Twins, Hunahpu and Xbalanque, descended into Xibalba to avenge their father, Hun-Hunahpu, and uncle, Vucub Hunahpu, who were tricked and killed by the lords of the underworld. They faced numerous trials and challenges, using their cunning and magical abilities to outwit the death lords. Their journey represents a cycle of death and rebirth, and their victory over Xibalba symbolizes the triumph of good over evil. The twins eventually defeated the lords of Xibalba in a ballgame and escaped the underworld.

keyMyths.title: The Death of Hun-Hunahpu
keyMyths.description: Hun-Hunahpu and Vucub Hunahpu were invited to Xibalba to play a ballgame against the lords of the underworld. They were tricked and defeated, resulting in Hun-Hunahpu's decapitation. His head was placed in a calabash tree, which miraculously bore fruit that resembled his head. This event is crucial because it leads to the miraculous birth of the Hero Twins and their quest for vengeance.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humanity
keyMyths.description: After the Hero Twins defeated the lords of Xibalba, they resurrected their father, Hun-Hunahpu. With the help of Ixmucane, the grandmother of the Hero Twins, humans were created from maize. This marked the beginning of the current age of humanity in Maya cosmology, showing the interconnectedness of the underworld and the creation of life.

sources.author: Dennis Tedlock

sources.text: A comprehensive translation and analysis of the Popol Vuh, providing insights into Maya mythology and cosmology.

sources.text: A general overview of Maya civilization, including their religious beliefs and mythology, with references to Xibalba.
